data.title: Nebius: Hypergrowth, Early-Stage AI Cloud Leader With Upside Potential
data.description:
Nebius (NBIS) is an AI cloud infrastructure disruptor. Although the stock surged by nearly 300% over the past 12 months, it remains highly undervalued if AI demand continues and tailwinds materialize.

A $3 billion partnership with Meta, $375 million pledge from Uber, and a multi-billion dollar agreement with Microsoft fuel a bullish narrative and highlight Nebius' market-leading position in the pioneer space of AI HPC.

The global AI data center market was valued at $17.73 billion in 2025, and is anticipated to quadruple in size by 2032, reaching $93.60 billion. This represents a 26.83% CAGR, and I think NBIS is well-positioned to benefit from broader market growth.

I rate Nebius a Strong Buy with a $130 price target over the next year. This presents a roughly 30% upside possibility and broader market outperformance. I think NBIS may appear to be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors seeking expanded exposure to the high-growth market of AI HPC.

FQ3 2025: Beat On EPS, But Miss On Top-Line

On November 11, the company delivered FQ3 2025 results. This was a solid quarterly performance, highlighting a beat on the EPS estimate but a miss on the top-line result. The market reacted to the results with a roughly 8% stock price decline over the following trading session.

Nebius posted $146 million in revenue, but $13 million below what the market had anticipated. The bottom line arrived at negative $0.40 in diluted EPS and $0.12 above analysts' anticipation.

In the shareholder letter, management highlighted that Nebius continues to execute well, justified by significantly raised power capacity guidance. The cloud pioneer anticipates more than 2.5 GW in contracted power, a substantial increase from the previously guided 1 GW. In addition to this, NBIS expects 220 MW in connected power by the end of the year, which could be used immediately after GPU installation.

This metric is anticipated to surge to approximately 900 MW at the midpoint in CYE 2026. I think this indicates a significant performance, well-managed business, and scalability, which deserves a premium valuation. If the trend continues, it will heavily reduce execution and conversion risks tied to $8 billion in expected run-rate revenue, supported by the above-highlighted contracts.

Nebius indicated that it has sold all available capacity, validating my bullish narrative tied to significant AI-powered demand. The company achieved a 19% adjusted EBITDA margin for its core AI infrastructure business, underscoring a 400% revenue growth on a year-over-year basis, and 40% on a quarter-over-quarter basis. This suggests a strong momentum in demand.

In addition to this, on November 12, the company highlighted it expects to deploy the first phase of Meta's deal already in December, which fuels a bullish narrative and supports significant revenue growth in the future. This is important because if NBIS's services meet hyperscalers' demand and expectations, this could lead to a more sticky customer base and potentially more deals in the future.

The AI infrastructure play gained approximately $4.16 billion in proceeds from bond convertible issuance over the first nine months of FY2025, and they will be used to accelerate scale and ramp up capacity. With the investments into GPU, infrastructure, land, and power, I think NBIS meaningfully reduces execution risk and positions itself to dominate in the future years.

I think this has been a solid quarter for the company. Execution remains intact, it anticipates significant growth in power capacity, and has meaningful contracts, fueling the bullish outlook. If NBIS can scale fast enough, and the AI-powered tailwind tied to demand continues, I think Nebius may significantly benefit from broader market growth. The momentum in demand and momentum is strong, and it will be interesting to see whether it continues.

Nebius Is Cheap, If Growth Delivers

The company is trading at a forward P/E (GAAP) of approximately 110, reflecting a 3.6x premium versus peers at nearly 31x earnings multiple. However, as it remains a heavily top-line focused company, I think forward P/S appears to be a more compelling metric to analyze NBIS's valuation.

The forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales ratios of above 40x highlight a significant, more than 12x premium versus the sector medians of 3.38 and 3.36. It signals to me a few key things.

The market anticipates significant revenue growth. This means investors are willing to pay a substantial forward P/S ratio for the company, anticipating a surge in top-line. Based on the current analysts' estimates, NBIUS anticipates more than 500% revenue growth in FY2026, which would indicate the AI infrastructure company remains cheap, if growth delivers. On the other hand, if management struggles to execute, and revenue comes in weaker-than-anticipated, it may correct from a premium valuation.

On the upside, Nebius has a low-leveraged capital structure. The company has $4.57 billion in total obligations. NBIS also has $4.79 billion in cash; therefore, it fully offsets the debt. This is important, as it highlights that management has financial flexibility to use debt-related instruments if needed.

The company achieved a 94.41% working capital growth, representing a roughly 23x outperformance versus peers at 4%. This also supports NBIS's ambitions and implies an aim for scalability.

The AI infrastructure company also operates at above-market margins. The gross profit margin (TTM) of 59% represents a 20% premium versus peers at 49%. The AI-powered landscape is known to be tied to outstanding margins, and if Nebius can scale successfully, I would anticipate margin expansion.

To conclude, NBIS is a pioneering company in the AI HPC space. The industry is tied to significant tailwinds, and Nebius appears to be positioned to dominate over the following years and benefit from broader market growth. The company has significant contracts with large hyperscalers and experiences substantial demand, justified by the announcement that all available capacity to be sold out. It also has a low-leveraged capital structure, leaving financial flexibility for management. I think the trend may continue further, which makes it a compelling opportunity to consider for long-term investors.

Wall Street anticipates $3.35 billion in revenue for FY2026. This would represent a 5x growth versus the FY2025 top-line estimate of $556 million. Based on my analysis, I think NBIS remains well-positioned to achieve these targets.

Nebius has roughly 252 million in outstanding shares. It currently trades at more than 40x forward P/S versus the FY2025 revenue estimate, and 7.14x versus the FY2026 estimate. I think if the company can successfully scale, a 9.5x forward P/S, an approximately 3x premium versus peers, seems to be reasonable, highlighting a triple-digit percentage revenue growth. Thus, if we divide $3.35 billion FY2026 revenue estimate by 252 million outstanding shares, we arrive at $13.3 revenue per share. At roughly a 9.5x earnings multiple, this comes at roughly my price target of $130. Although I think it may come across as too conservative, I favor a cautious approach, as it leaves me room for error.

Main Risks And Concerns

Nebius is tied to risks and concerns that could meaningfully impact stock price performance in the short term.

The company is heavily dependent on AI-driven demand and sentiment. The broader markets remain concerned about the heavy CapEx investments from the Mag 7 companies. If investors anticipate that these investments will not translate to meaningful top and bottom line growth, sentiment may dramatically shift, and the AI theme could be heavily discounted.

Although Nebius has significant contracts, fueling a positive outlook, they come with an execution risk. So far, it seems that the company has successfully scaled and is guiding for immediate connected power of 220 MW by the end of the year. If the trend continues, and the company avoids any challenges or issues clouding the outlook, I think it positions itself for future market dominance. Yet the concern remains, and investors should keep an eye.

The AI HPC landscape is attracting companies to invest in and service cloud infrastructure services. It results in fierce competition. Nebius competes against CoreWeave (CRWV), the legacy Bitcoin miners, such as Iren (IREN), Cleanspark (CLSK), Cipher Mining (CIFR), and others, and that may pressure the company's performance in the long term. Although based on my analysis, the AI HPC market is anticipated to significantly increase over the following years, increased competition may weigh on margins and profitability. However, it seems that multiple names could successfully operate in the space.

NBIS is also sensitive to IT spending. Although this correlates to AI-driven demand, yet if the global economy enters a recession, the companies may reduce their spending, and that could cloud the future outlook.

On the other hand, Nebius appears to be well-positioned to ride the AI-powered wave. If the company succeeds in scaling, I think it could become a market-leading name in the years to come.

Nebius Is A Strong Buy

I think Nebius may appear to be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors seeking diversification into a high-growth, high-demand market, tied to AI-powered tailwinds. The company's outlook is supported by multi-year contracts with a few Mag 7 names, and that fuels a bullish narrative.

Nebius has a low-leveraged capital structure, trades at an appealing forward P/S, and actually may be cheap if growth delivers. The AI cloud infrastructure company continues to scale and guides for a positive and significant increase in capabilities. I think the company is well-positioned to benefit from broader market growth, especially if AI-powered demand continues.

I rate Nebius a Strong Buy with a $130 price target over the next 12 months. This presents a 30% upside possibility and broader market outperformance. 8 Wall Street analysts forecast a $163 price target over the next year, suggesting a 63% upside potential. Time will tell, but it will be interesting to monitor if expectations materialize.

data.title: Grand Canyon Education: Shares May Need To Consolidate For Valuation To Catch Up Somewhat (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Intro

Our most recent commentary on Grand Canyon Education, Inc. (LOPE) was in March of this year, when we downgraded the education & training company to a 'Hold'. Given the gains LOPE reported since our initial December 2023 upgrade, the March downgrade was the right call, given that the stock is approximately 9% down over the past nine months (compared with almost a 20% gain in the S&P 500).

Our commentary in March was a technical piece, which it will be this time around as well. Technical analysis typically works well in multi-billion-dollar companies (Grand Canyon Education's market cap currently stands at $4.43 billion) due to ample trading volume, which provides us with patterns to run our analysis on. As chartists, we believe that all known information that could potentially affect LOPE's share price (whether it be fundamental, psychological, industry-based, etc.) has already been embedded in the company's technical charts. Case in point: Our objective is not to try and outsmart LOPE's investing public here but instead to be aligned with the prevailing trend over time (strong hands).

Trailing EBIT Margin Below 5-Year Average

To the above point, we have stated previously that LOPE would need to continue to report buoyant top-line growth rates in order to justify the overhanging valuation currently on offer. The reason is that, considering where LOPE is presently in its investment cycle, cutting costs (thus, boosting margins) does not look likely. LOPE's operating margin, for example, came in at a mute 21.2% in the recent third quarter (resulting in a trailing average of 27.25%), whereas the company's 5-year average EBIT margin comes in at a higher 28.36%. This means the company's EBIT has only increased by just over 7% over the past five years, as opposed to a stock price that has rallied approximately 87%.

Enrollments in the GCU ground campus are Stagnant

Therefore, considering the poor performance of LOPE stock post its Q3 earnings release (Reported on 11/5/2025), when taking LOPE's growth channels as a whole, investors do not see enough scope for searing growth in the online campus, the GCU ground campus, Grand Canyon's Education hybrid campus, and the Centre for Workforce Development. Online enrollments decelerated in Q3 compared to the second quarter, with total enrollments in the GCU ground campus also reporting a negative growth trend due to FAFSA site complications and above-average summer school graduations. Management, as we see below, explained the reasons behind the poor growth levels in the traditional college campus segment and that LOPE stands to gain (through its other platforms) from how the 'traditional student' has been branching out to the areas.

The fact of the matter, however, is that there is no guarantee that growth in the likes of LOPE's online & hybrid segments will directly benefit from the ground campus struggles. Furthermore, with marketing spend remaining elevated and buybacks on the wane, strong forward-looking EBIT growth rates will remain difficult to achieve, all things remaining equal.

As we move forward, there are 3 trends that are impacting traditional college campuses throughout the country. Number one, the number of high school graduates on an annual basis continues to decline. Number two, the percentage of high school graduates who are choosing the 4- or 5-year baccalaureate path continues to go down, while the number of students choosing shorter certificate or trade programs is going up. Three, the number of high school graduates choosing a baccalaureate path, but doing it fully online, also continues to go up. We're in a very strong position given these trends. We have a high-quality, affordable offering on the GCU ground campus, but we have even greater program choices for students who want to go fully online or to move back and forth between ground and online.

Long-Term Technical Chart

As we see on LOPE's long-term chart below, given both the height of the MACD and the corresponding bearish crossover in this indicator, all odds now are on at least a test of the depicted multi-year trade-line below. MACD crossovers are especially noteworthy on long-term charts due to the dual role of the indicator, along with the sheer amount of data that makes up the signals. A similar bearish MACD crossover back in 2018 led to years of lower prices before shares finally were able to rally above those 2018 highs five years later in 2023.

Intermediate 7-Year Chart

As we see below, heavy selling pressure has resulted in a breach of the depicted trendline, which dates back to late 2022. Given the oversold nature of the intermediate histogram, we expect some sort of bounce over the next few weeks, maybe back up to the depicted trendline at over $170 a share. However, given LOPE's 10-week moving average has just crossed below its 40-week counterpart, this 'death cross' could easily be a precursor to more near-term selling in this play, all things remaining equal.

Short-Term 12-Month Chart

What is concerning for LOPE on its short-term chart is that the downside gap, which took place in early November, will provide further overhead resistance if indeed shares manage to regain the depicted trendline below. The turning down of LOPE's 200-day moving average is also noteworthy, considering it had been rising aggressively until late October. Such significant changes demonstrate a clear change in investor sentiment, which is why we do not recommend buying into weakness here, irrespective of how oversold the stock may appear.

Conclusion

To sum up, we are reiterating our 'Hold' rating in LOPE due to concerning technicals, margin struggles, and muted growth rates, especially in enrollments with respect to the GCU ground campus. Although by no means a 'Sell' due to sound profitability, investor sentiment remains weak, which is why caution is required. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: Dell: The Infrastructure Powerhouse That Will Profit From The AI Boom
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I see Dell (DELL) as a bullish case because the company is well-positioned to profit from the AI boom. Rather than developing models, the company is in a position to provide much of the needed hardware and cloud infrastructure for companies that are training and deploying these models. More specifically, its dominance in the server market offers a major growth opportunity that can reward investors who are willing to buy into its most recent dip in price. As evidence of this, the company has done a major revision of its long-term guidance for financial growth as demand in the AI space continues to rapidly increase.

Dell's Current Strategy

Dell has made it clear that a large part of its future plans involves capitalizing on the current AI boom through its servers and cloud infrastructure. In fact, its AI shipments grew by 150%, which paves the way for a clear path forward for the company moving forward. This is largely based on the company's unique ability to develop and deploy large-scale AI clusters with its server technology. Furthermore, it has an ecosystem that is hard for competitors to match while also having flexible financial holdings. These two factors combine to give the company a substantial moat that allows it to profit from the ongoing AI development boom.

Dell's Partnerships And Ecosystem

A key component of Dell's strategy is developing its partnerships and further integrating users into its ecosystem. One of these key relationships for the company is its agreement to integrate Nvidia (NVDA) into its high-performance servers. Additionally, the company is working with Elastic (ESTC) to accelerate its data infrastructure buildout.

While Dell's AI strategy is largely based on its hardware advantage, it is still pursuing other options to get in on the data game. One example of this is the company's development of its own AI data platform. This is designed to help enterprises with streamlining distributed but siloed architectures. As a result, the company is investing heavily in expanding its specialized tools that help clients organize, query, and use AI models. I personally feel that this aspect of the business's strategy is being overlooked, as it is a key issue that large-scale clients need.

Dell's Future

The Company's Record Orders And Backlog

The company's efforts to capitalize on the AI boom are showing results. It currently has a major backlog in terms of its hardware products. This has resulted in its AI server orders reaching a record of 12.3 billion. Additionally, Dell's server business generated approximately 45% of its TTM due to AI-based orders. In particular, this was from neoclouds, enterprises, and even sovereign spending. This shows that this part of the business is extremely robust and growing.

Dell's Collaborations

A major contributor to Dell's potential future is its collaborations. One of the biggest contributors to its future is its current partnership with Nvidia and UT Austin. This project seeks to build one of the largest supercomputers in the US. This supercomputer can help to establish Dell as a leader in this space, as long as it is successful in achieving its performance metrics.

Dell's Fiscal Overview

Despite Dell's reduction in overall profitability, the company is currently performing well in terms of profitability and debt reduction. These are factors that are hard to ignore when looking at the company's future. Its trailing month of $104 billion is over 10% growth from the previous year. This number is far higher than the company's five-year average, and I feel this accelerating growth for the enterprise is a significantly bullish sign for the future.

While the company's growth in revenue is impressive, it is trailing its rivals in the trailing twelve months of Gross Profit Margin. Here, the company has a margin of over 20% in stark contrast to the sector median of 49%. I see this as showing Dell as operating in a highly competitive environment and cutting prices in order to overpower its rivals.

Despite Dell's lower margins, the company is keeping its costs low. This suggests that its cost management efforts are proving to be highly effective. Its TTM Net Margin of 5% slightly exceeds the sector median of 4.72%. As a result of this slight advantage, the company recorded a TTM Net Income of over $5.2 billion.

While the company has impressive cash flow, it is also a highly indebted enterprise. This has to be a key focus for potential investors in the company. It currently has over $9.6 billion in Cash and Equivalents. This may seem healthy, but it must be compared against the company's $31 billion in Total Debt, as a result, the company's TTM Net Debt is over $21 billion. A TTM Current Ratio below 1.0 shows that the company's short-term assets cannot cover its short-term liabilities. Dell's is currently standing at 0.85. While this would normally be troubling for new investors, the substantial financing that Dell provides makes this lower ratio more manageable. This is especially true given that the company has been aggressively investing in future growth opportunities.

Another key aspect to keep in mind with Dell is its cash flow as it remains a core strength of the company. Its TTM Cash from Operations was an impressive $7.09 billion. This is a healthy amount compared to its CapEx of $2.64 billion. I see this as a substantial pool of money that the company can use to further drive itself forward while still giving shareholder returns. The company currently has a Levered Free Cash Flow of -1.44%. This may raise a red flag to investors, but I see it as an impact of its current working capital and debt payments. However, its current cash generation ability is more than capable of managing these obligations for the enterprise.

A major element of the company's strength is its core Cash from Operations, which was an impressive $7 billion. Even when I account for the company's CapEx of $2.6 billion, this figure shows further proof of the funds available for debt servicing, expansion, and shareholder turns. This is a critical factor in my giving the company a bullish rating, as it indicates that its revenue and profit growth are outpacing the debt the company has accumulated in its efforts to grow.

Setting A Potential Price For Dell

Despite Dell's challenging debt structure, it has achieved impressive top-line growth, and management is effectively controlling costs. While I believe that the stock is primed for upside potential, I must evaluate it in a more quantitative way. Since I view the stock as more of a long-term investment option, I will use its consensus two-year estimates to help guide my evaluation.

To this end, the company's growth is driven by its major investment cycle, in particular, in AI server demand. For the period that ends in 2027, the Target EPS is $11.44. The next step is to find a reasonable target P/E multiple to use. Given the company's recent growth, I believe that the company deserves a bit of a premium over the sector median. As a result, I want to assign it a P/E of 15x, which is slightly over the Forward P/E of 13.48x. This offers a bit of safety for the assumptions while dealing with the company's rapid earnings expansion.

With these assumptions, it is possible to create an expected price for the company moving forward. To this end, I will multiply the Target EPS by the Target P/E multiple. This results in $11.44 times 15, which provides us with a potential price of $171.60. I feel that this bullish figure shows the major upside that the company potentially has for the next couple of years. These calculations may be a little too optimistic, as they reveal that there is an over 30% upside to investing in it, but even if these calculations are slightly off, the company's growth in earnings and revenue shows that it is in a strong position to gain value moving forward.

Dell's Primary Risks Moving Forward

Memory Prices

AI is notorious for the amount of memory that it requires, and this is leading to increasing prices for memory as hyperscalers purchase it in bulk. This has the potential to compress Dell's margins as it will have to spend more on NAND and DRAM. This is a major threat to the company moving forward, as Dell is considered severely vulnerable to this risk.

Weakening Traditional Business

While the company is positioning itself to be a major player in the AI boom, it is facing weakness in its traditional business of computer sales to clients. To this end, the segment of the business saw a quarterly decline of 0.2% and a year-over-year decline of 7%. These numbers suggest that it may be too focused on the AI boom at the risk of the core business that has made Dell so successful over the decades.

AI Profitability Worries

Dell is positioned to be a major player in the AI infrastructure space. However, its success in this space is largely dependent on AI providers and services being able to turn a profit on their products. If this does not happen, then demand for Dell's servers and cloud infrastructure will decline, which could greatly impact revenue and pricing power. This is a space that should be watched closely by investors, as it is unclear how long the AI buildout will last or even the lifespan of the chips and servers these companies are buying.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I am extremely bullish on Dell. This is despite the potential headwinds the company is facing, as it is well-positioned to be a key supplier in the AI boom that is ongoing. Its current price seems to be undervaluing the company, as my calculations suggest there is a potential for the stock to reach $171, which is a 30% upside for its current trading prices. While investors will want to continue to monitor the AI buildout for signs of slowing, it is currently in an extremely strong position.

data.title: Salesforce Q3: The Agentforce Inflection Point
data.description:
The fresh Q3 earnings release of Salesforce (CRM) reinforces my bullish opinion about this company. CRM demonstrated strength across all key financial and business metrics, which helps the company to drive robust EPS growth. The cash flow generating capacity is also improving as the company remains strong in converting accounting profits into value for shareholders. I feel optimism from the management’s comments during the earnings call, meaning that CRM has potential to unlock new growth catalysts in the foreseeable future. The stock price remained approximately at the same level since my previous bullish article, meaning that valuation remains very attractive. All in all, I reiterate “Strong Buy” rating for CRM.

Fresh Q3 earnings review

CRM has just released its Q3 earnings, which I consider solid. Headline number did not disappoint compared to consensus estimates for the third consecutive quarter, which is likely to improve investors confidence in CRM. I think so because it is always safer to invest in a company that is able to consistently outperform against consensus estimates. Being an earnings surprise champion does not guarantee robust rally, but underperforming against consensus estimates usually trigger sell-offs. Therefore, another quarter with double-beat is a large bullish factor for me.

Revenue grew by 8.7% YoY, which is approximately in line with previous quarter’s performance. While revenue growth is stable in high single digits, CRM’s bottom line is demonstrating aggressive dynamic. The company’s non-GAAP EPS grew from $2.41 to $3.25 on a YoY basis, which implies an impressive 35% growth compared to the same quarter last year. I pay a lot of attention to the factors that triggered EPS expansion because it helps to assess fundamental quality of the bottom line growth. The picture looks good for CRM because such a strong EPS growth was fueled by operating leverage as the company’s non-GAAP operating margin expanded from 33.1% to 35.5% YoY. As a result, cash generating capacity continued improving as well with the FCF growth rate of 22%. And I think that these profitability and FCF generation improvements are likely to continue on the grounds that CRM’s remaining performance obligation [RPO] demonstrated strength in Q3 with a double-digit 12% YoY growth. All the data that I mentioned regarding margins, FCF, and RPO were obtained from the earnings presentation.

We don't have a 10-Q report yet, but I think that due to the 22% FCF growth and the management’s historically prudent capital allocation, CRM’s balance sheet highly likely remains strong because the company had $3.5 billion net cash position as of the end of Q2. With CRM's $2.2 billion free cash flow during third quarter, it is highly probable that the company's cash pile has accumulated notably by the end of Q3 compared to the end of Q2 [$15.4 billion in cash and cash equivalents by the end of Q2]. The improving financial position is vital for shareholders because CRM is well-known for its generous buyback programs. Moreover, dividend payouts were initiated relatively recently, and the improving balance sheet positions CRM to deliver robust dividend growth over the long-term.

From the future growth standpoint, having a strong financial position is a formidable asset for CRM because the company is well-known for its comprehensive growth strategy, which requires substantial capital to invest in R&D and M&A. I have high conviction in CRM’s potential to generate value from investments in growth not only because of its A+ profitability but also due to the management’s comments during the earnings call that mentioned a few very interesting potential growth catalysts.

First and foremost, the company's biggest project of the AI era, the Agentforce, is gaining traction. According to the earnings call transcript, the ARR of Agentforce skyrocketed by staggering 330% YoY during the quarter. Such aggressive growth means that CRM's AI-powered value proposition is really compelling and creates value for users. The management said that they are "relentlessly reallocating resources" to this high growth area, which is already paying off. I think that with a big early win like a 330% YoY growth, CRM's proven track record of success, and aggressive R&D investments in this area, we will likely see new releases related to this product. This will be a great opportunity for CRM to cross-sell new features to its vast customer base.

Last but not least, the management also raised guidance across all key income statements except for the GAAP operating margin [a 90-bps downgrade] and the non-GAAP operating margin [remained flat]. Such a confident guidance is mostly backed by the rapidly growing Agentforce business, which implies the company's ability to capitalize on AI tailwinds. With such a strong performance, confident outlook from the management, and quite promising strategic initiatives in the pipeline, I think that CRM is well-positioned to create more value for shareholders over the long-term.

Valuation update

The stock is down by 29% YTD, which brought the company’s market cap to around $220 billion. Valuation ratios are substantially lower compared to CRM’s historical averages. I am not comparing to the sector median because CRM is a unique and highly profitable company that remains the number one player in the customer relationship management software market for more than a decade.

Additionally, CRM’s EPS is projected to maintain double-digit growth over the next few years, which means that significant P/E ratio contraction is expected. Besides low current valuation ratios compared to historical ones and the expected rapid P/E ratio contraction, Wall Street analysts tend to agree with me that CRM is substantially undervalued.

The consensus target price for CRM is $328, implying a large 37% upside potential. I know that average target prices from Wall Street analysts do not always mean that the gap between it and the actual price will close rapidly. However, there is a quite reliable way to cross-check the consensus price. To do so, we need to multiply the upgraded EPS guidance by the benchmark P/E ratio. Let's say that we are ultra-conservative and take the sector median forward P/E as a multiple [even though CRM boasts premium profitability compared to peers and a clean balance sheet]. I am doing this ultra-conservative calculation just to show how big the margin of safety is. The revised non-GAAP diluted EPS projection from the management is $11.76 and the sector median forward non-GAAP P/E ratio is 23.68. Multiplying these two numbers gives me $278.5 which is around 15% higher compared to the current share price.

Risks to consider

Sentiment around CRM remains quite weak despite the company’s fundamental strength, which I see from the muted market’s reaction after impressive quarterly performance. Regaining investors’ confidence is not an overnight process and it might take a few more quarters of stellar execution before the market’s sentiment around CRM recovers 100%.

We also see that the entire stock market experiences weakening sentiment because of the fears related to valuations of companies with AI exposure. It relates to both public companies like semiconductor and cloud giants and to private generative AI companies like OpenAI or Anthropic. If the broader market continues demonstrating cautiousness, it is difficult to expect a share price spike from CRM even if fundamentals continue improving.

Besides fears related to valuation of AI companies, there are fears related to broader macroeconomic context. The U.S. 10-year treasuries yields are still above 4%, which is another factor that might keep investors nervous. Moreover, there are recession risks, trade wars, and geopolitical uncertainty. The latter two might be real headwinds for CRM if the landscape becomes less favorable for the company’s international expansion. In addition, expanding international footprint will mean foreign exchange risks, which should also be considered before making the investment decision.

The bottom line

To conclude, I think that CRM remains a “Strong Buy”. The company’s financial performance is stellar, and it is backed by strong performance across key business metrics. New projects look promising and increase probability of discovering new sustainable growth drivers.

data.title: SCHD ETF Alternative Strategy Aimed For Higher Total Return
data.description:
4-Factor Dividend Growth Portfolio

My 4-Factor Dividend Growth Strategy is an alternative investment strategy to the popular Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). You can think of it as having your own custom version of SCHD tailored to your personal investing style. Let me tell you how it works and the results it has attained thus far.

The strategy was launched on November 1st, 2022. You can read about the original strategy, stock selection process, and portfolio construction in this 4-Factor Dividend Growth Portfolio article. In a nutshell, the strategy leverages the stock selection process of Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF, or rather its underlying index, the Dow Jones US Dividend 100 Index (DJUSDIV), with a few minor modifications.

Since I prefer a more growth-oriented investment style relative to what SCHD's underlying index offers, I built my own custom investable universe to which I apply a slightly modified stock selection process. This is a fully automated strategy with no manual decision-making on my part.

My personal portfolio tracking this strategy is rebalanced with newly identified stocks on November 1st of each year. You can read more about the most recent rebalancing in this article.

Stock Selection Strategy

The strategy has a simple objective: to identify potentially high quality companies that stand out amongst their peers across 4 criteria:

  1. Free Cash Flow to Total Debt Ratio
  2. 5-Year Dividend Growth Rate
  3. Return on Invested Capital
  4. Forward Dividend Yield

The thought process behind these specific criteria is also quite simple. I favor highly liquid, low-leveraged companies that have a history of generating high returns on their invested capital and high dividend growth. And I prefer to focus on companies that also sport better-than-average dividend yields. Since each of the 4 metrics is equally weighted, the companies that stand out amongst their peers are generally not the best in any single metric but instead score well across all 4.

Before we get too far into the weeds, let's rewind and address the "potentially high quality companies" statement I made earlier. The 4 criteria would do a good job of identifying potentially higher-quality companies, but the process can be enhanced by starting with a universe of already potentially high-quality companies.

To accomplish this, I run a stock screener each month that provides me with a short list of companies that all possess characteristics of high-quality companies.

Here are the criteria I search for:

  1. Exchange: NYSE or NASDAQ
  2. 3-Year Dividend Growth Rate: >5%
  3. 5-Year Dividend Growth Rate: >5%
  4. Payout Ratio - TTM: <80%
  5. 5-Year Revenue Growth Rate: >5%
  6. 5-Year EPS Growth Rate: >5%
  7. Morningstar Moat: Wide or Narrow
  8. Morningstar Stewardship: Exemplary or Standard

This set of criteria typically yields a short list of 120-130 companies that are chosen for the investable universe. From here I apply my 4 criteria inspired by SCHD's stock selection methodology. The top 20 ranked stocks make the cut to be included in the portfolio. To follow a similar strategy as SCHD, the chosen stocks are held for 12 months, after which the portfolio is rebalanced with newly chosen stocks.

Track Record

I've been tracking this strategy on a monthly basis since June 2023, with my actual portfolio launched on November 1, 2022. I'm tracking the lists on a monthly basis to expand my dataset for a more robust analysis.

Thus far 19 unique lists have completed a full 12-month cycle, generating an average total return of 11.6%. My long-term target is 12%, so the strategy is currently a bit short of its intended goal. However, SCHD's average total return for the same time period is 10.93%, placing my strategy 0.66% ahead.

The 12 pending lists, January 2025 through December 2025, that have yet to complete a full 12-month cycle have an average total return of 7.22%. While this total return is far lower than the 12% target, these lists still have a handful of months to complete their full cycle. For perspective, the average total return for comparable SCHD portfolios is 1.33%, placing my strategy a cool 5.89% ahead, on average.

Looking at the performance results for individual months, here are the current winners:

  1. December: 15.88%
  2. June: 14.78%
  3. November: 12.53%
  4. July: 11.89%

Thus far only two months are underperforming SCHD:

  • March: 5.73% vs. 6.42% = -0.69%
  • May: 5.99% vs. 6.04% = -0.05%

The widest margins are:

  • June: 14.78% vs. 9.00% = 5.78% alpha
  • July: 11.89% vs. 7.04% = 4.85% alpha
  • January: 11.68% vs. 7.21% = 4.48% alpha
  • December: 15.88% vs. 11.90% = 3.98% alpha

Latest Modification

More recently, starting in July 2025, I've started testing if incorporating an expected rate of return to the stock selection process would yield better long-term results.

The expected rate of return = Forward Dividend Yield + 3-5 Year EPS Growth Forecast + Return to Fair Value Component.

The return to fair value component is measured by analyzing a 5-year price-to-free cash flow ratio and comparing it to the trailing twelve-month price-to-free cash flow ratio. The under- or overvaluation is then annualized over 5 years, based on the reversion to the mean principle.

The expected rate of return is used as a final screening filter applied to the original ranking process. The top 20 stocks with an expected rate of return of at least 10% are included in the portfolio.

Thus far the 6 pending lists chosen with this modified method have mixed results.

data.title: Flowco Holdings: A Downturn In Oil Is Exactly What This Company Wants
data.description:
Thesis

Flowco Holdings (FLOC) is an oil and gas field service provider that actually benefits from the recent overall decline in sector profitability. The company offers downhole and surface equipment that boosts overall production at a time when operators are pushing hard to optimize their existing portfolios.

The company offers high-pressure gas injection systems, vapor recovery technology, as well as traditional gas lift equipment that can either be rented or bought outright. The majority of the company’s revenue and profit comes from rental fees that allow the customer to increase overall production. As a result, the well operator achieves a net financial benefit from employing FLOC’s services.

A relationship with FLOC allows operators to save precious capital budgets while also maximizing FCF following the decline in crude oil prices. This creates a win-win relationship where FLOC generates business by helping its customers maximize what is perhaps the most valuable metric to Wall Street.

Win-win relationships are rare. When they are combined with an attractive valuation, the opportunity becomes bullish. However, FLOC’s ownership structure is complicated and favors the founding investors over the common shareholder.

I recommend a BUY rating for those comfortable with the relationship and the risks of a minority ownership position.

What Does Flowco Offer?

FLOC offers a variety of downhole and surface-level components aimed at improving total production from the customer’s wellbore. The company offers a full suite of artificial lift systems in addition to vapor recovery systems to maximize the output of a well.

The main attractions here are the company’s High Pressure Gas Lift and Vapor Recovery applications. Beyond these core products, the company also offers traditional gas lift applications that are used later in a well’s operating life.

For those unfamiliar with High Pressure Gas Lift systems, these systems utilize high-pressure natural gas that is injected into the wellbore to both thin and help carry the fluid stream out of the reservoir. YouTube has multiple examples for those looking to learn more about the method.

This type of system is designed to be an improvement over the traditional ESP equipment (Electric Submersible Pump). The advantage of the HPGL system is that by using pressurized natural gas, the required flow rates can be throttled depending on the point in time of the life of the well.

In contrast, using an ESP over the entire production decline curve will create both capital and operational inefficiencies over the life of the well. As many know, shale wells experience significant volume declines over the first two years of life. A pump needs to be sized accordingly to handle the high flow rates at the beginning of life. However, flow rates fall exponentially. This makes selecting an appropriately sized pump for the life of the well an impossible task.

After about two years, any pump sized to produce the initial peak flow rates is likely running at just a fraction of its designed capacity and at a very inefficient point on its operating curve if left in service. This results in both higher electricity consumption and wasted capital resulting from buying a larger capacity pump.

Thus, implementing the HPGL provides more efficient operation during the highest production period of the well's life. When HPGL is no longer effective, FLOC can then provide the customer an opportunity to switch to an ESP, thanks to its broad product offerings.

The ESP in this application will be more appropriately sized for the volumes of the mid-years of the well's life. This allows for more efficient operation of the ESP and lower capital costs for the operator. In this way, FLOC aims to be a lifelong partner with producers by being able to offer multiple years of revenue over the life of a well.

In addition to downhole offerings, FLOC also provides surface-level equipment that, again, is focused on optimizing total well production. The company’s vapor recovery system aims to recover methane and entrained NGLs from the well site’s production stream. This is similar to capturing the gasoline vapors in your car’s gas tank.

FLOC deploys these systems under both rental and sale contracts to offer customers the maximum flexibility possible. FLOC estimates that customers generate a payback in just 2-6 months, making this product offering another win-win for the industry as a whole.

A Cyclical Downturn Is Just What FLOC Needs

It's no secret that margins in the oil patch are down from when crude traded above $75/barrel. As a result, operators are focused on optimizing their portfolios to become more efficient. Many operators are reporting significant progress in improving capital efficiency through faster drilling times and higher production.

Hate it or love it, FCF is how Wall Street puts value on the E&P space. Thus, all of these efficiency efforts are aimed squarely at producing more with less invested capital. This helps to preserve FCF in a lower-margin environment.

The service sector is the most likely candidate to face the brunt of this squeeze, as they are the first call on capital spending. However, FLOC's service offerings play well for a countercyclical business.

As discussed earlier, FLOC offers the majority of its components through either a rental or sales arrangement. For the producer, it is much more advantageous to rent this equipment in order to preserve precious FCF. Every million that slips out of the capital budget is sure to be highly scrutinized by Wall Street. By rotating this spend into the OPEX bucket, operators can effectively preserve FCF while taking a minimal per-barrel hit thanks to FLOC's production enhancing services.

As a result, I expect FLOC to gain rental market share even if the industry shifts to more of a maintenance mode. I believe this is possible through two key methods. The most obvious method of gaining market share is through increased industry adoption. The company expects the market share of both its HPGL and VRU units to grow by over 10% by 2030.

The second driver is that FLOC's business is more directly affected by total wells in service than total industry volumes. By being capable of offering services throughout the life of the well, the addressable market becomes larger as more wells become active, even in a flat volume environment.

By being able to grow market share in a sector that is still expanding the total well count, FLOC has solid prospects of expanding its high-margin rental fleet. This should easily offset any EBITDA contraction in the low-margin product sales segment.

On the top line, the rental fleet generated 60% of total revenue in Q3. In addition, this rental fleet generated a 72.6% margin. By comparison, the sales segment generated a profit margin of just 35.8%. The current business environment presents the opportunity to swap one-time sales opportunities for multiple recurring high-margin rentals. I think this is a trade-off any business owner would want to make.

Valuation

Since the company's IPO in 2024, FLOC's valuation has followed the market's bearishness on the oil and gas industry. The service sector has been hit extremely hard, as one would expect this segment to bear the brunt of the activity slowdown. As a result, FLOC’s share price is down 43% YTD.

When valuing FLOC, the company’s ownership structure complicates the calculation. As shown below, the company has issued 27.4 million Class A shares of common stock of Flowco Holdings. The Class A shareholders own a 30% economic interest in Flowco LLC. The remainder is owned by "redeemable non-controlling interests."

These interests are carried by Class B shares (which are not entitled to dividend payments). This separation impacts both reported net income to common stockholders as well as basic vs diluted EPS.

Since these interests are redeemable for Class A stock, it is reasonable to value all equity at the current share price. In doing so we arrive at a combined market cap of Class A and Class B shares of roughly $1.53B. If one annualized the EBITDA generated in Q3, FLOC trades at roughly 5.7x EBITDA.

This places FLOC at a slight premium to other service providers. The exception here is LBRT, whose share price has lately been caught up in AI mania due to the company leaning into its power production segment.

However, the key distinction between FLOC and these peers is that none of the three companies listed here operate under a rental business model. A more direct comparison for this model would be the natural gas compression market. In this market, compressor units are rented on multiple-year contracts to both producers and midstream customers to support their operations.

In the chart below, we can see that this group trades at a significant premium to service providers due to the contracted business structure. Because FLOC’s business appears to be growing away from product sales and more in the direction of equipment rentals, I believe this is more of an apples-to-apples comparison. In this context, FLOC appears undervalued as it continues to build out its rental business.

Risks

Beyond the obvious risks of engaging in a cyclical market, I believe FLOC’s ownership structure is likely the biggest deterrent to shareholders. The structure is admittedly complicated and provides different taxation rules between the two ownership classes.

With only a 30% stake available to the Class A shareholders, the common investor will always be in the minority. The original ownership group of Flowco LLC will continue to have controlling voting power over the business.

The only way for this to change would be for an owner to exercise the "redeemable" clause of their ownership interests. In doing so, new Class A shares would be issued, thereby diluting the Class A shareholder pool. While this does not affect the fundamentals of the business, such a transaction could potentially challenge the share price temporarily.

Investor Key Takeaways

1. Flowco provides production-enhancing services that can be deployed over the entire life of the well.

2. In doing so, the total addressable market is any active well. This makes the business less influenced by active rig counts or total production volumes.

3. The company derives a significant portion of its profits from its rental business. The higher profit margin generated by this business should more than offset any decline in product sales.

4. The current cyclical downturn bodes well for FLOC to boost its rental business. FLOC’s rental products produce win-win situations for customers by enhancing production and preserving FCF through CAPEX savings.

5. The company is valued at a premium to typical oil field service providers but also at a discount to businesses with a more comparable business model. As the company’s rental fleet grows, the company should be more closely valued to the natural gas compression business, providing multiple expansion opportunities for the stock.

6. I rate FLOC as a BUY.

data.title: Evolv: Prepared To Jump Back In As Conditions Improve (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Background

Evolv Technologies Holdings, Inc. (EVLV) is a 12-year-old security technology company (although it started selling its products and services only from 2019 onwards) that specializes in AI-powered screening and analytics solutions that are used to detect weapons. These solutions are primarily utilized in markets such as education, hospitals, live events, and industrial workplaces, amongst others.

We are of course not new to the EVLV counter and have covered it a couple of times now. Our most recent coverage was back in August 2025, a few days before the company reported its Q2 results. Until August we had maintained a bullish stance on the stock (for roughly 16 months in total, during which time the stock moved up by around 85%), but then we pivoted to a hold.

Since our last coverage, EVLV has lost positive traction, and is down by over 12%, even as its tech peers have moved up by over 9%, and its Russell 2000 counterparts have fared even better, with double-digit percentage gains during the same period.

Nonetheless, since it’s been almost 4 months since we last reviewed this counter, and the stock has also witnessed a healthy retracement, we thought it would be a good time to take stock of things and see if EVLV is worth pursuing again, or is it still better to wait on the sidelines.

Transition From Distribution Channel To Direct Fulfilment Should Result In Better Long-term Economics

The last time we covered EVLV, we touched upon the importance of focusing on ARR [annual recurring revenue] progression (which is currently growing at a pace of 25% YoY), which we felt would be more fitting for a business that was becoming more subscription-oriented. In recent months, EVLV has taken steps to transition to a new go-to-market model (which has been largely completed as of mid-November) that could result in even better dynamics on the ARR front, as well as other metrics.

To elaborate, previously, EVLV’s non-leasing purchase model involved customers either directly buying their hardware from them (direct purchase subscription), or through an intermediary- the distribution partner- and then getting into an associated 4-year subscription term. When the intermediary was involved, EVLV was facing lumpy revenue readings, lower contract revenues of 70%, limited deal velocity, a longer sales cycle, and limited control over pricing and services; crucially, less than 50% of the customer order was getting converted to EVLV’s ARR.

Now, under the new direct fulfillment model, they are weeding out the distributor (the intermediary), with channel partners purchasing the product directly from EVLV and making things more streamlined (this will also help during renewal season). Crucially, EVLV is now locking in 100% of the contract values, converting a higher threshold of customer orders (65-70%) to their ARR, getting better visibility on their RPOs (remaining performance obligation), ensuring better forecasting, and also generating higher cash per unit sold.

Encouraging Progress On Other Key Metrics As Well

Speaking of cash, even before this shift in the go-to-market model, EVLV was already seeing a pickup in its operating cash flow profile, driven in part by tighter inventory management (which has proven to be a source of cash to the tune of $11M this year).

Note that historically, EVLV has failed to generate any annual operating cash inflow during the last 6 years, but this year, in 2 out of the 3 quarters, they have managed to buck the trend, and on a 9M basis, cash inflows are coming in at $3M (whereas this time last year, they were seeing a cash drag of $34M).

We believe inventory management and the shift in the go-to-market model will continue to beef up EVLV’s operating cash flow position, with the company likely to see its first full year of positive operating cash flow by the end of December.

One reason why we are more optimistic about EVLV’s long-term inventory management is because they are broadening their supply chain, and early last month they got into a new contract manufacturing tie-up with another company besides their legacy supplier-Columbia Tech called Plexus Corp.

Superior cash generation is also coming from EVLV’s growing ability to keep a lid on its cost base while also extracting superior leverage. Note that this year, in all the 3 quarters, OPEX growth has been trending lower on a YoY basis.

It has also meant that this year they are generating positive EBITDA to the tune of over $9M (whereas for the first nine months of last year, EBITDA was negative to the tune of -$21M), with margins coming in at high single digits.

This has also prompted EVLV to increase their EBITDA margin guidance for the FY from previous expectations of mid-single-digits to high-single-digits.

It’s worth noting that consensus expects these EBITDA margins to keep improving, and two years down the line it could be closer to 11%.

All in all, if EVLV can continue to generate an increasing cadence of cash flow through organic operations, rather than relying on cash from debt (it raised $26M in Q3 through this avenue), it should be taken well, as the cash balance on the books, which was at over $300M a few years back (accounting for over 80% of the asset base), had been on a declining trend but bottomed out in Q1-25.

Moving on from cash, we are also encouraged to see consistent signs on the customer addition front, with EVLV now adding 62-63 new customers a quarter (in total they have over 1000 customers and looking to close 8100 subscriptions by the year's end).

EVLV isn’t just adding new customers; they also have a good chunk of customers renewing and upgrading to the Gen2 Evolv Express systems, which is also resulting in the expansion of the overall RPO [Remaining performance obligation] pie, a leading indicator of future revenue trends.

Note that earlier in the year, the RPO was showing signs of abating but has picked up meaningfully over the past couple of quarters, and is now almost approaching $300M!

Risks

EVLV’s shift in its go-to-market model is likely to cause some near-term headwinds on its gross margin profile. Under the earlier model, EVLV would only receive a licensing fee with the one-time COGS [cost of goods sold] being recognized on the distributor’s books; now with the distributor out of the way, EVLV will have to recognize the non-recurring COGS on its books, while the subscription revenue will be spread out over a larger period, causing pressure on gross margins.

Some investors may also be put off by EVLV’s pricey valuations of over 90x EBITDA; since it is only going to generate positive EBITDA by the end of this year (management guidance points to $11-$13M), the denominator is not yet sturdy enough to justify the $1B plus EV [Enterprise value] which has also gone up because of the added debt that EVLV raised in July.

What Do The Charts Suggest?

One of the reasons why we had pivoted from a buy to a hold in August was because the risk-reward on the charts did not look very favorable. Back then, the stock was hugging its upper Bollinger Band (which is two standard deviations away from the 20-week rolling average), and was coming up against an old resistance. Now that scenario has flipped with the stock witnessing a healthy correction towards it lower Bollinger Band (a mark of selling over-extension given that it is two standard deviations away from the 20-week rolling average) and forming a double bottom (which typically signals a reversal from a downtrend) at the $5.5 level.

What we also appreciate is that EVLV has managed to defend and bounce off its 200 DMA (something it has been defending since April 2025) on the daily chart, which is widely perceived to be the most important moving average for gauging bullish and bearish conditions. Note that the direction of the 200 DMA is also still trending higher.

Investors may also want to take note of the relatively diminished appetite from the short-selling community (the short float stake, which was at 16% levels at the turn of the year, is now at a manageable threshold of just mid-single-digits).

Finally, also consider that the EVLV’s relative strength ratio versus other options from the broad tech universe is currently perched at a 33% discount to its long-term mean, and thus could potentially witness some upward mean reversion momentum.

Closing Thoughts- Rating Revision

After maintaining a HOLD for the last 4 months, we are now prepared to once again turn more sanguine about the EVLV stock. We appreciate the company’s pivot on its go-to-market model, which should galvanize the ARR and cash flow profile, while it is also adding a healthy cadence of customers (and also seeing renewals) and contracts, resulting in a larger RPO pie. Management is also keeping a good lid on costs, with positive annual EBITDA almost on the anvil, with double-digit EBITDA margins not too far away. We also feel that the stock does not look overbought like it did the last time we pursued it, and the more favorable risk-reward position provides further confidence to shift our rating back to a BUY.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SuRo Capital: CoreWeave Dips As AI Enthusiasm Moderates
data.description:
SuRo Capital's (SSSS) position in CoreWeave (CRWV) is set to be closed out at a markedly lower realized gain following the collapse of the cloud computing company. This might see near-term returns for the equity BDC capped after a remarkable year-to-date AI-driven run. CRWV is down roughly 60% from its 52-week high, with the BDC holding a $37 million position at fair value in CRWV as of the end of its fiscal 2025 third quarter. This formed 14.7% of its $252.2 million investment portfolio, with SuRo's net asset value ("NAV") at $9.23 per share at the end of the third quarter. NAV per share expanded by 5 cents sequentially from $9.18 per share in the second quarter. This growth from when I last covered the ticker came ahead of what's expected to be another $0.25 per share dividend distribution to common shareholders. This will mean SuRo is set to pay out $0.50 per share in aggregate to its common shareholders in 2025 as it continues to ride the AI wave.

The company's portfolio is broadly in good health. WHOOP, its second-largest holding at 11.4% of its portfolio, recently signaled an intention to take the health tracking wearables company public by 2027. This comes as OpenAI's valuation jumped to $500 billion on the back of a secondary share sale. SuRo has kept its valuation of the AI company at the previous $300 billion post-money valuation, which could mean more upside to NAV in the event OpenAI's valuation jumps again on a future funding round. Critically, the outlook for SuRo's common shares will hinge on AI animal spirits amid concerns of a bubble as a broad risk-off sentiment drives AI companies and relevant pick-and-shovel plays to pull back from recent highs. NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) has been shedding some value from its highs, with Oracle (ORCL) also losing 42% of its market cap from its highs, totally eliminating the premium the market had attached to the company following its $300 billion deal with OpenAI. SuRo has proved more resilient, with its stock price anchored to third quarter NAV per share at what's currently a moderate 1.08% premium.

Net Asset Value, Investments, And Exits

SuRo's position in OpenAI forms 11% of its investment portfolio and should be the primary position in driving future value for the BDC. The BDC sold some of its CRWV shares post-period end for net proceeds of $7 million and a realized gain of $5.3 million and retains roughly 71.8% of its investment as of its third-quarter earnings release on November 4, 2025. The cloud computing company would see its market value drop precipitously in the weeks following. SuRo is likely to have reduced its stake further, as it's not in the business of owning positions in public companies. There was also a $1 million realized loss from an investment in Rebric (d/b/a Compliable). This matched SuRo's initial investment, as there were no net proceeds received.

A core risk for shareholders coalesces around the extent to which CRWV will continue to dip. AI angst continues to rise as credit markets get nervous around debt-driven data center spending. This has already increased financing costs for AI firms and is pushing down sentiment around listed AI firms. For SuRo, this limits a possible expansion of its current premium to NAV while also raising the specter of a reduction in the value of its private AI bets. AI euphoria has powered the stock to a record high, and a popping of bullish sentiment could push it markedly the other way.

OpenAI is also facing greater competition from Anthropic and Google (GOOG), a long-term threat to its existing half-a-trillion-dollar valuation. This comes after SuRo focused its third-quarter investment activity on investing in the preferred shares of HL Digital Assets. This provides SuRo's investors exposure to HYPE, the digital token of decentralized exchange Hyperliquid. HYPE is a tradable cryptocurrency that's dipped from its recent high on the back of the pullback in the crypto markets. The exchange has performed strongly, generating $500 million in revenue year-to-date as of the end of the third quarter, as it looks to expand beyond derivatives trading. It's a risky investment, but it reflects the type of high beta play that SuRo needs to continue to ramp up its NAV.

Conclusion

The sale of the CRWV position is still being completed at a healthy premium to the initial cost, despite the dip in the company stock price. However, a further pullback in AI sentiment represents downside for SuRo's common shares and NAV. Despite the limited position at $5 million, the investment in HYPE also represents a specter of risk that some shareholders are likely not going to be comfortable with. SSSS remains a hold.

data.title: Enterprise Products Vs. Energy Transfer: Both 'Buy' As Complementary Assets
data.description:
Investment thesis

One of the key areas of focus for investors today is the energy sector. This is especially true for investors who are focused on generating high passive income. The anticipation of a recovery in oil and natural gas prices is strengthening the US's position as a leading global player. The Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) and Energy Transfer (ET) are the undisputed leaders in the midstream sector, increasing the investment attractiveness of their assets. These MLPs manage a large-scale US transportation and energy infrastructure, critical during the AI boom and the construction of data centers.

Over a month ago, I focused separately on the fundamental analysis of EPD and ET. These assets each have strong individual strengths, leading me to assign them a "Buy" rating. While I am optimistic about the strategic growth potential of the midstream sector, it's important for me to conduct a direct comparative analysis to answer the question: which one of these companies is the most attractive for my investment portfolio? This article will analyze their fundamental similarities and differences to help you understand which of these MLPs offers the best risk-return profile for you. Using the data I gathered, I have come to a final conclusion, identifying only one of these giants for inclusion in my investment portfolio.

Overview of Enterprise Products Partners and catalysts for its growth

Enterprise Products Partners is one of the largest operators in the US market, providing energy transportation services (mainly gas). In doing so, there are a number of products that enable operations across the entire value chain (from natural gas processing to shipping via marine terminals for export). The EPD EBITDA structure consists of 82% fees, whereas 13% is differentiated income derived from the gas price spread between points of sale, with the remaining 5% coming from commodity-linked components.

The Enterprise Products Partner production and transportation infrastructure consists of storage facilities with a capacity of 300 mln bbl, an 80,000 km oil pipeline, 45 natural gas processing lines, and 27 operating liquid hydrocarbon plants. Currently, EPD is implementing projects to build facilities for transporting and processing large volumes of LNG and ethane. Especially important is the development of ethane-related capacities, for which global demand is growing significantly, as EPD has a competitive advantage in this area. Enterprise Products Partners owns two of the largest ethane and ethylene terminals in the United States. For instance, in July 2025, the Neches River ethane terminal in Texas, with a capacity of 120,000 bpd, was commissioned. A second phase of the Neches River terminal is expected to come online in early 2026, adding 180,000 bpd of capacity. The EIA estimates that US ethane exports will ultimately grow by 14% in 2025 and 16% in 2026.

EPD's total investment project portfolio includes financing of $6 billion. Among them, for instance, are the Permian Basin gas processing plant project and the 14th LNG rectification column in Mont-Belvieu. The Bahia Pipeline project plays an important role, enabling the transportation of liquid natural gas fractions from the Permian Basin to Chambers County, home to EPD's natural gas liquid fractionation complex. EPD is not only doing its own projects, but it's also really into M&A deals. For instance, in 2024, it acquired Piñon Midstream, which helped it grow its natural gas gathering and processing business in the Delaware Basin (deal value: $950 million). Also, EPD is buying Occidental's gas gathering subsidiary, which will allow it to build a new gas processing plant in the Midland Basin. The assets include natural gas gathering facilities at more than 1,000 drilling locations and 200 miles of pipelines. The transaction is valued at $580 million in cash.

Overview of Energy Transfer

Energy Transfer, like Enterprise Products Partners, acts as one of the largest operators in oil and gas transportation. Its total pipeline length is 140,000 miles. The structure of its EBITDA consists of 90% fees, where a balanced distribution between sources of cash flow is formed. The bulk (27% of revenue) comes from the supply of liquefied natural gas and petroleum products. Around 21% of revenue comes from domestic and international transportation fees. Transportation of crude oil and natural gas accounts for 20% and 19%, respectively. The other 13% comes from a number of affiliated and subsidiary companies, like Sunoco and USA Compression.

Growth catalysts for Energy Transfer include current demand for natural gas and liquefied natural gas in the US and abroad. This is expected to increase due to the impact of the technological boom in the development of artificial intelligence technologies. The ET Assets include approximately 105,000 miles of natural gas interstate and intrastate pipelines, approximately 236 Bcf of natural gas storage, and expertise in dual-drive gas/electrical compression. EIA forecasts that demand for electricity in the US will grow to 4,305 billion kWh in 2026. This provides incentives for projects to build new power plants, where natural gas plays an important role.

As U.S. LNG exports grow, Energy Transfer is involved in new infrastructure projects that let them ship LNG to export terminals, from where it's sent to customers all over the world. For instance, the Lake Charles project is currently being retooled to export approximately 16.5 million metric tons of LNG per year. Chevron, China's ENN Energy, and South Korea's SK Gas Trading are among the customers that have signed long-term contracts to purchase LNG from the Lake Charles terminal. Due to rising costs, however, the final investment decision is expected to be made in Q1 2026.

Fundamental similarities

Enterprise Products Partners and Energy Transfer are among the most popular assets in the energy transportation sector. Their facilities infrastructure is so extensive that it is virtually impossible to imagine the US energy system without access to these assets. EPD and ET are classified as MLPs, allowing US residents to enjoy tax benefits (deferred taxation on distributions). Therefore, most investors consider them to be interchangeable assets in their portfolios, though there are important differences between their strategies. Defining their similar qualities, it's worth noting that they have a similar business model. The two companies earn commissions from the transportation of energy resources. Nearly 90% of their revenues come from fees for the use of the energy resource transmission pipeline system to electricity producers, making this source of cash flow stable during periods of high volatility in hydrocarbon market prices. But this does not mean that they are completely immune to price risks.

But a deeper look at the basic similarities between EPD and ET helps us figure out four main things that make them different. There are differences in business strategy, assets, dividend policy, and valuation.

Criterion #1. Business strategy

I would categorize the differences between EPD and ET in terms of business strategy as conservative management (in the case of EPD) and aggressive management (in the case of ET). The bottom line is that EPD is a company focused on organic growth. Management has historically made decisions aimed at implementing its own projects so as not to overpay for other projects (by acquiring them through M&A deals). Speaking of ET, the current management of the company (led by Kelsey Warren) has often made decisions to acquire other assets. Due to this, ET's production and transportation infrastructure network is growing at a faster pace. But this has its price, related to an increase in debt burden.

Criterion #2. Assets

It is important to evaluate EPD and ET assets with a focus on their product portfolio. Speaking of EPD, it should be noted that, first of all, they are a leading company in the NGL sector. The assets are structured to create a value chain from the well to the plastic. The EPD's assets play an important role in creating the company's export potential. Particularly, as I mentioned earlier, the EPD's main interest is centered around the prospect of increasing its share of the global ethane market. As far as ET is concerned, their extensive network of gas pipelines in most US states allows them to balance their portfolio between LNG and natural gas. They are also focused on exporting their products, but as part of the LNG business.

Criterion #3. Dividend policy

What I want to highlight here is how EPD and ET payouts differ in terms of reliability. The fact is that Enterprise Products has been increasing its payouts for 27 years now. In fact, this makes them the dividend king in the MLP sector. In spite of the financial difficulties that followed during COVID-19 (in 2020), they still continued their payments, not reducing them, but raising them. The Energy Transfer is not such a stable player in terms of payments to investors. In that same 2020, they conducted a 2-fold reduction in their payments. The main task at that time was to make debt repayments. Since that case, ET's payments have increased. Still, this story has left a mark on its reputation. Maybe to get rid of the negative impact of this event, today ET makes higher payments than EPD (7.9% vs. 6.7%).

Criterion #4. Valuation

The differences in the companies' assets and the fact that ET has higher debt mean that Energy Transfer trades at a higher discount than EPD. For instance, ET's P/DCF multiple is typically lower (~5x-6x) than Enterprise Products' (~8x-9x). On one hand, this is positive, because ET trades at a discount, making it more attractive in terms of growth expectations. At the same time, it reflects the assessment of potential risks, with EPD's assets appearing more reliable. Although, according to a number of other metrics from the Valuation group, they are very similar.

Fundamental factors for growth

Identifying the fundamental factors for the potential growth of EPD and ET in the foreseeable future, I want to mention the main reasons separately for each company.

Enterprise Products: The key factor driving its value growth in 2026 is an increase in cash flow following the completion of major infrastructure projects. Capital expenditures will be reduced, which will allow the company to earn more and spend less on construction processes. Within the framework of its quarterly report for Q3 2025, management has already emphasized that it expects a sharp increase in free cash flow next year. Among other things, it can be used for share buybacks or another increase in dividends. Also, EPD is building up its dominance in NGL exports among American producers. Implementation of the project to launch the new Neches River terminal is expanding export capacity. Due to this, EPD's share of the global ethane market will increase.

Energy Transfer: The major factor driving its value growth in 2026 is its involvement in the development of data centers, more specifically, in providing them with energy resources to power their computing capacities. As part of its quarterly report for Q3 2025, management announced new contracts to supply gas to Oracle and Fermi America data centers. The ET gas transportation network is ideally located to provide data centers with access to energy resources. Hence, the AI boom factor has a more significant impact on ET than on EPD. Energy Transfer is also expanding its presence in the Permian Basin. In spite of delays with the Lake Charles LNG project, its remaining production assets are operating at full capacity. This allows us to expect even greater volumes of oil and gas supplies next year, driven in part by growing demand in the US domestic market.

Risks of the investment thesis

Determining the risks of recommending EPD and ET, it should be noted that the main threat remains price risk, as the potential decline in the cost of natural gas and oil could reduce companies' revenues. Despite the fact that up to 90% of their EBITDA consists of fees, deteriorating economic conditions in the global and US energy markets could lead to a reduction in orders for hydrocarbon supplies. Among the reasons for this, for example, could be macroeconomic instability. It particularly carries additional risks for the growth of EPD and ET prices during the AI boom. Recent news about the potential collapse of the "AI bubble" could lead to a significant reduction in electricity consumption forecasts (due to lower computing power in data centers). Ultimately, this would lead to a decline in the consumption of energy resources such as natural gas, which has a 42% share in US electricity production.

Conclusion

In summary, after analyzing the characteristics of companies such as Enterprise Products Partners and Energy Transfer, I find them to be highly attractive investments. I maintain my previous recommendation of "Buy" for both companies. At the same time, I consider these two assets to be complementary and believe that they have significant potential for growth in value during the recovery period for energy resource prices. In choosing between these assets, I would go with EPD, since it is a more reliable company that has proven its effectiveness during the most difficult periods for the energy sector. Additionally, I have faith that Enterprise Products will be able to realize its strategic growth potential by increasing ethane exports to the global market. If you're not as risk-sensitive and want an asset with a higher dividend yield, ET is the obvious choice. That said, this company is influenced by the same fundamental factors that are driving its strategic growth.
